coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical sensor for the detection of dopamine using carbon quantum dots/copper oxide nanocomposite modified electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Elugoke, Saheed E.
Fayemi, Omolola E.
Adekunle, Abolanle S.
Mamba, Bhekie B.
Nkambule, Thabo T.I.
Ebenso, Eno E.
coredata.dc:description: This paper presented a facile fabrication of an electrochemical sensor from carbon quantum dots CQDs and copper oxide (CuO) nanocomposite for dopamine detection. Individual components of the sensor and the composite were characterized with UV–visible, FT-IR, EDS, XRD spectroscopy, and microscopic methods such as the SEM and TEM. The nanocomposite (CQDs/CuO) was applied for modification of glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The electroanalysis of DA at CQDs/CuO nanocomposite modified GCE showed that DA detection can best be achieved at about 0.3 V. The detection limit of this sensor (GCE/CQDs/CuO) determined via square wave voltammetry (SWV) was 25.40 µM over a wide linear range of 1–180 µM. This sensor also demonstrated outstanding reproducibility even though it has relatively low stability. The percentage recovery of dopamine from dopamine hydrochloride injection obtained with this sensor confirmed its suitability for DA detection in pharmaceutical samples and possibly some other dopamine-containing real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical sensor
Carbon quantum dot
Nanocomposite
Square wave voltammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs)
Characterization of the nanomaterials and nanocomposite
Electrocatalytic response of CQDs/CuO to dopamine (DA) oxidation
Effect of pH on DA oxidation
Effect of scan rate on DA oxidation at CQDs/CuO modified electrode
Dopamine redox reactions and scan rate changes: Experimental and theoretical studies
Electroanalysis of DA at CQDs/CuO modified GCE

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of CuO nanoparticles, CQDs and CQD/CuO nanocomposite.
UV spectra of (A) CQDs, (B) CuO nanoparticles and (C) CQDs/CuO nanocomposite.
XRD diffractograms of (A) CuO NPs and (B) CQDs and (C) CQDs/CuO.

SEM micrograph of (A) CuO nanoparticles (B) CQDs (C) CQDs/CuO nanocomposite and (D) particle size distribution chart from the TEM micrograph of CQDs. EDS spectrum of (E) CQDs and (F) CuO nanoparticles.
Cyclic volatmmogram of bare GCE, GCE/CQDs, GCE/CuO and GCE/CQDs/CuO in 10 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox probe in PBS (pH 7.0) at a scan rate of 25 mV s−1.

(A) Cyclic voltammogram, (B) graphical representation of anodic current response of bare GCE, GCE/CQDs, GCE/CuO and GCE/CQDs/CuO towards 0.4 mM DA in PBS (pH 7.0) at a scan rate of 25 mV s−1 and (C) cyclic voltammogram of bare GCE and GCE/CQDs/CuO in PBS (pH 7)).

(A) EIS spectrum (Nyquist plot) of the bare and modified electrodes in 0.4 mM DA in PBS (pH 7) at a scan rate of 25 mV s−1 (inset: enlarged Nyquist plots) (B) Equivalent circuit for fitting GCE/CQDs EIS data (X) and the EIS data of other electrodes (Y).

(A) Cyclic voltammogram showing the effect of pH and (B) Graphical representation of current response of GCE/CQDs/CuO to pH change in 0.4 mM DA in PBS (pH 3–9) at a scan rate of 25 mV s−1 (inset shows the variation of potential with pH change).

(A) Cyclic voltammogram showing the effect of scan rate on DA peak current at GCE-CQDs/CuO modified electrode, (B) The plot of anodic and cathodic peak current against the square root of scan rate and (C) The plot of anodic and cathodic peak current against the scan rate.
A chart of (A) UV absorbance of DA against DA concentration (10–100 µM) and (B) changes in absorbance with increase in scan rate (25–300 mV s−1).
Optimized (A) dopamine and (B) dopamine-o-quinone structures. Frontier molecular orbital energy distribution (HOMO and LUMO) of (C) dopamine and (D) dopamine-o-quinone.
(A) Square wave voltammetry (SWV) and (B) linear plot of the current response of GCE/CQDs/CuO to DA concentration changes from 10 − 180 µM at a scan rate of 25 mV s−1 in 0.1 M PBS.

Cyclic voltammogram of 5 mM AA and 0.2 mM DA using (A) Bare GCE and (B) GCE/CQDs/CuO. Differential pulse voltammogram of (C) 5 mM AA and 0.2 mM DA and (D) 1 mM AA with DA concentration of 100–125 µM using GCE/CQDs/CuO (scan rate = 25 mV s−1 and pH = 7).
Cyclic voltammogram of 0.2 mM dopamine at GCE/CQDs/CuO recorded at a scan rate of 25 mV s−1 (pH 7) over 20 days at 5 days interval.
The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy parameters at various electrodes.
Dopamine and dopamine-o-quinone quantum chemical parameters.
Comparison of figures of merit of previous sensors with the present work.
Percentage recovery from real sample analysis.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical sensor for the detection of dopamine using carbon quantum dots/copper oxide nanocomposite modified electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nanotechnology and Water Sustainability, College of Science, Engineering and Technology, University of South Africa, Johannesburg 1709, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper presented a facile fabrication of an electrochemical sensor from carbon quantum dots CQDs and copper oxide (CuO) nanocomposite for dopamine detection. Individual components of the sensor and the composite were characterized with UV–visible, FT-IR, EDS, XRD spectroscopy, and microscopic methods such as the SEM and TEM. The nanocomposite (CQDs/CuO) was applied for modification of glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The electroanalysis of DA at CQDs/CuO nanocomposite modified GCE showed that DA detection can best be achieved at about 0.3 V. The detection limit of this sensor (GCE/CQDs/CuO) determined via square wave voltammetry (SWV) was 25.40 µM over a wide linear range of 1–180 µM. This sensor also demonstrated outstanding reproducibility even though it has relatively low stability. The percentage recovery of dopamine from dopamine hydrochloride injection obtained with this sensor confirmed its suitability for DA detection in pharmaceutical samples and possibly some other dopamine-containing real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine is one of the most studied neurotransmitters (NTs). This could be attributed to its significance as a neurotransmitter (NT) with the capacity to coordinate essential activities of the entire human system such as learning, behavior, emotion, and movement [1]. The imbalance in the concentration of this biomolecule has been implicated in chronic diseases like Parkinson’s disease, dementia, epilepsy, schizophrenia, among others [2,3]. The fabrication of electrochemical sensors for dopamine detection has been attempted using various materials.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are one of the environmentally benign alternatives to the inorganic CQDs made from metallic precursors with very high cytotoxicity [4]. They are zero-dimensional nanomaterials with very small particle size and appealing optoelectronic properties such as high electrical conductivity, large surface area, good photoluminescence lifespan, and fluorescence quantum yield [4,5]. CQDs have also been applied as a component of electrochemical sensors to detect various analytes both individually and as members of composites. For example, Han et al. [6] reported the preparation of CQDs from xylan and its incorporation into Ag nanoparticles and reduced graphene oxide in order to obtain a composite for DA detection. The high sensitivity recorded at the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified by this composite was ascribed to the modified electrode’s high catalytic activity and conductivity. Also, CQDs prepared from Carica Papaya juice via the hydrothermal route were combined with polyaniline (PANI) for DA detection in the presence of ascorbic acid as an interfering molecule [7]. The authors confirmed that the GCE modified with PANI/CQDs composite was capable of discriminatory DA detection in the presence of AA with wide peak separation. The CQDs nano-size and the porous nature of PANI, which encourages the trapping of CQDs, were identified as the reasons for effective DA sensing at the PANI/CQDs composite. Similarly, electrochemical detection of low levels of DA has been achieved with an electrochemical sensor fabricated by electrospinning PANI/CQDs on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) [8]. Huang et al. [9] prepared a composite made of selenium-doped CQDs (Se-CQDs) and β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) for DA, uric acid (UA), and AA detection. The ability of the GCE modified with this composite to detect DA, UA, and AA simultaneously was attributed to the synergy between Se-CQDs and β-CD.

Metal oxide nanoparticles have a considerable reputation for combining with carbon nanomaterials to yield composite with high sensitivity towards DA detection. Composites of this nature have benefitted from the high conductivity, catalytic activity, and biocompatibility of the metal oxide nanoparticles and the large surface area and high electrical conductivity of the carbon nanomaterials [10]. For instance, copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) composite has been synthesized for DA detection [11]. The Au electrode modified with this composite displayed better sensitivity towards DA compared to CuO NPs modified Au electrode and the rGO modified Au electrode. The synergy between rGO with large surface area and the highly conducting CuO NPs was ascribed to the superior performance of the Au electrode modified with this composite. Similarly, the higher DA and UA oxidation current recorded at the Fe2O3-GO modified GCE relative to the current response at Fe2O3/GCE or GO/GCE was attributed to the synergy between Fe2O3 nanoparticles and grapheme[12]. In the same vein, CQDs prepared through sucrose carbonization were added to cuprous oxide nanoparticles (Cu2O) via sonication in the presence of nafion (as a binder). The success of GCE modified with this composite was ascribed to the conductivity of Cu2O and the electrostatic attraction between DA and the anionic CQDs (and nafion) at physiological pH [13]. The authors also confirmed that the carboxyl groups on the CQDs surface and the sulfo group on nafion improved DA detection in the presence of AA and UA by favourably attracting DA. Successful DA detection achieved using these composites and previous carbon nanomaterials/metal oxide nanocomposites found in literature inspired the current study. In addition, this study strives to combat the toxicity that characterizes the adoption of inorganic quantum dots for sensors fabrication while exploiting the conductivity and biocompatibility of the relatively cheaper CQDs.

Herein, the determination of DA with CuO NPs and CQDs nanocomposite has been attempted. This was inspired by the low bandgap and the attendant high electrical conductivity of CuO NPs and the biocompatibility of CQDs. The CuO NPs were prepared via chemical precipitation technique and incorporated into a composite with CQDs prepared from the carbonization of pencil graphite. To fabricate the sensor for this study, this composite (CQDs/CuO) was used for GCE modification. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on electrochemical DA detection with an electrode composed of mainly CQDs-CuO nanocomposite as the chemical modifier.

Copper sulfate pentahydrate (98% CuSO4·5H2O), ethanol (99%), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (99.99 % NaH2PO4), disodium hydrogen phosphate (99 % Na2HPO4), dopamine hydrochloride (98 %), ascorbic acid (99 %), dopamine hydrochloride injection (200 mg/5 mL), hydrofluoric acid (75 % HF), potassium permanganate (97 % KMnO4), hydrogen peroxide (35 % H2O2), sodium hydroxide (99 % NaOH), dimethylformamide (99.8 % DMF), potassium hexacyanoferrate (III) (99 % K3Fe(CN)6), potassium hexacyanoferrate (II) (99 % K4Fe(CN)6). Sigma Aldrich Chemicals, Pomona AH, Kempton Park, 1619, South Africa, supplied all chemicals. All reagents were of analytical grade. HB pencils were procured locally.

The CQDs were prepared from a pencil graphite precursor according to Hummer’s method adopted by Algarra et al. [14] with slight modification. The pencil graphite was manually extracted from the HB pencil with a knife. About 1 g of the graphite was dispersed in a beaker containing a mixture of 60 mL conc. HF and 6 g KMnO4. The resultant mixture was refluxed at 100 °C for one hour in a Teflon-lined autoclave. This mixture was allowed to cool at room temperature prior to the addition of 10 mL hydrogen peroxide. The dark brown suspension obtained was diluted with distilled water and centrifuged for 10 min at 4000 rpm to separate the fluorescent QDs from the less fluorescent sediment. The CQDs obtained were dried in an oven for 6 h at 90 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs)
ce:section.ce:para: Copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs) were prepared through the chemical precipitation technique adopted by Hwa et al. [15] with slight modification. Briefly, 0.2 M of CuSO4·.5H2O and 0.1 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were prepared separately in a 250 mL capacity conical flask. About 80 mL of the as-prepared NaOH solution was placed on a magnetic stirrer and heated simultaneously at 80 °C for 30 min. This was followed by dispensing 50 mL of as-prepared copper sulfate solution from a burette in drops. The resultant solution was heated further for another 10 mins while stirring. The mixture containing the CuO NPs was allowed to cool and stand for 8 h. The CuO NPs were separated from the solution and transferred into an oven at 80 °C for 12 h.

About 4 mg of the as-prepared CuO NPs and CQDs of the same weight were transferred into glass containers containing 0.2 mL DMF. The mixture was ultrasonicated in a water bath at 25 °C for 24 h. The resultant paste was used for the modification of GCE by drop casting.

Firstly, the glassy carbon electrode was cleaned using alumina slurry prepared on a sic-emery cleaning pad. The cleaned GCE was sonicated first in distilled water and finally in ethanol. This was followed by rinsing the electrode with distilled water and subsequent drying in the dry air oven at 50 °C for 3 min. Secondly, the GCE was modified using the drop-cast method. The as-prepared CQDs/CuO nanocomposite was deposited on GCE and dried in the oven at 50 °C for 30 min. The modified electrode was used for the electroanalysis of dopamine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the nanomaterials and nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The nanomaterials (CQDs and CuO NPs) and the nanocomposite (CQDs/CuO) were characterized using spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The UV–visible spectroscopy was done with Uviline 94,000 UV spectrophotometer (with a wavelength range of 200–700 nm) supplied by SI analytics, Germany. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was done with Quanta FEG 250 microscope supplied by ThermoFisher Scientific, USA. Sample preparation in ethanol and subsequent deposition on a solid substrate (mica) were carried out before SEM analysis. The operational parameters such as the contrast, brightness and working distance of the microscope were adjusted. The spectra for the energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDS) accompanied that of the SEM. The functional groups in the materials were analyzed with a Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometer procured from Bruker Corporation, USA. The FT-IR spectrophotometer (Opus Alpha-P) was applied within a wavelength range of 400–4000 nm. X-ray diffractograms were obtained with Rontgen PW3040/60 X’Pert Pro diffractometer at 2° min−1 scan rate and diffraction angle of 10° − 90°. All electrochemical measurements were done using an Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat PGSAT-20 operating on Nova 1.3 software and an electrochemical cell with a three-electrode system. The reference, working and counter electrodes were Ag/AgCl, platinum wire, and CQDs/CuO modified GCE, respectively. Electroanalysis of the analyte and electrochemical characterization was done in phosphate buffer saline solution (0.1 M PBS, pH 7) prepared from Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4.

The FT-IR spectrum of CuO showed absorption bands at 485 and 688 cm−1 which correspond to the Cu–O stretching vibration. The absorption band at 3382 cm−1 suggests the presence of –OH functionality due to the moisture present in the as-prepared CuO nanoparticles [16,17]. This CuO FT-IR spectrum is identical to the spectrum obtained by Chaudhari [16] for CuO prepared from copper nitrate trihydrate precursor. The FT-IR spectrum of CQDs revealed absorption bands at 1086 and 3202 cm−1, suggesting the presence of C-O and –OH functional groups, respectively. The broad –OH group can be ascribed to the hydroxyl group of –COOH functional group present in the CQDs due to the oxidation of surface aliphatic –CH groups in the graphite starting material. The absorption band at 1639 cm−1 appeared due to the presence of C = C stretching vibration in the graphitic core, while the weak peak at 1046 cm−1 is due to the presence of C-O stretching vibration originating from C-OH. The peak at 718 cm−1 is due to the C-F bond present due to the introduction of HF into the graphite flakes. The absorption peaks at 3020, 1742, 1101 cm−1 on the FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs/CuO nanocomposite suggest the presence of –OH, C = O and C-O. The weaker and broader (relative to CQDs spectrum) –OH stretch at 3020 cm−1, and the weaker absorption band at 485 cm−1 (relative to that of CuO) are indications of the interaction between the CuO nanoparticles and CQDs. The reduction in the intensity of the peaks in the CQDs after composite formation suggests a reduction in the quantity of oxygen-containing functional groups [18]. It could also be inferred that the persistence of prominent peaks of the CuO nanoparticles after composite formation suggests the stabilization of CuO nanoparticles in the composite [19] (see Fig. 1 ).

The CQDs UV spectrum (Fig. 2 (A)) revealed a peak at 290 nm due to the n- π* transition, which occurred due to the presence of the C = O functional group [20]. The UV spectrum of the CuO nanoparticles (Fig. 2 (B)) showed two absorptions at 290 and 355 nm. These absorptions have been reported for CuO nanoparticles in literature [21]. The strong absorption at 290 nm and the weak absorption at 355 nm represents the surface plasmon absorption maximum for CuO nanoparticles [22]. The combination of CQDs and CuO nanoparticles for composite formation reinforced the weak peak at 355 nm (which shifted to 430 nm) while the peak at about 290 nm in the individual materials shifted to 304 nm. This bathochromic shift confirms the formation of a material (nanocomposite) whose interaction with the UV light is entirely different from that of the individual nanomaterials.

The XRD pattern of the CuO nanoparticles and CQDs can be seen in Fig. 3 . The diffraction angle (2 Theta) and the corresponding miller indices (in parenthesis) for the signals corresponding to the diffraction pattern of CuO nanoparticles were 32.7° (110), 35.5° (002), 38.7° (111), 48.8 (202), 53.7 (020), 59.3° (202), 61.6° (113), 66.2° (311), 68.0° (113), 72.5° (311) and 75.2° (004). These peaks correspond to the diffraction pattern for the CuO nanoparticles’ monoclinic phase. A similar diffraction pattern for highly crystalline CuO nanoparticles has been reported [23]. The average crystallite particle size obtained from Debye-Scherer’s equation calculated from the prominent assigned peaks on the diffractogram is 14.6 nm. The diffractogram in Fig. 3 (B) showed several peaks due to the presence of carbon dots and impurities in the CQDs sample. In essence, the peaks at 26.57° and 42.3° symbolized the presence of graphitic carbon [24]. More importantly, the strong peak at 26.57° (a diffraction angle greater than 21.1°) indicates that the CQDs probably contain coarse carbon nanomaterials [20]. The interplanar spacing (d) calculated from Bragg’s equation (Eqn (1)) using the peak at 26.57° (θ = 26.57/2, n = 1 and x-ray wavelength, λ = 0.154 nm) is 0.34 nm. This value of d is close to the value reported for graphitic carbon (0.33 nm), suggesting that the CQDs maintain a level of crystallinity similar to that of graphitic carbon [20]. The blue plot in Fig. 3 (B) represents the nanocomposite’s (CQDs/CuO) XRD spectrum. This diffractogram showed that most of the peaks on the CuO NPs spectrum are no longer present in the composite, even though those at 35.5° (002) and 32.7° (110) remained intact. In addition, the peaks originally on the CQDs spectrum reduced in intensity and position when found in the composite (Fig. 3C). Specifically, the peak at 26.57° in the CQDs spectrum reduced in intensity and shifted to 27.9° in the composite. This drastic change in the composite’s XRD spectrum is an indication that a new material (CQDs/CuO) with an entirely different pattern of interaction with the x-ray source (diffraction pattern) has been formed.
(1)
2 d S i n θ = n λ

The SEM micrograph of the nanomaterials and the composite in Fig. 4 shows that the CuO nanoparticles are majorly spherical particles with randomly distributed particle sizes. The particles appeared compact with only a few sparsely placed particles. The compactness of these particles can be confirmed by some parts of the micrograph (Fig. 4 A), revealing very dense layers of CuO nanoparticles (blue circles). The SEM micrograph of the CQDs (Fig. 4 B) revealed arrays of closely parked (fused) carbon flakes with small portions of irregularly shaped particles.

The irregularly shaped particles are probably coming from the carbonaceous impurities in the CQDs. These impurities are unreacted graphite particles from the starting material. The SEM micrograph of the CQDs/CuO nanocomposite (Fig. 4 C) showed that the carbon flakes CQDs particles are surrounded by the spherical CuO nanoparticles (red circles). Interestingly, these nanocomposites, just like the CuO nanoparticles, were formed in layers, even though these layers are not as dense as seen in CuO nanoparticles micrograph. This confirms possible van der Waal interaction between the CuO nanoparticles and the CQDs. The average frequency density (AFD) calculated from the particle size distribution of the CQDs in the TEM micrograph (inset: Fig. 4D) is 9.02 ± 0.18 (Fig. 4D). This implies that the CQDs have an average particle size of 9.02 nm. This particle size agrees with the particle size of <10 nm reported for carbon quantum dots [1,14]. The energy-dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDS) spectrum of the CuO NPs (Fig. 4 (E)) and the CQDs (Fig. 4 (F)) shows the elemental composition of the materials. The EDS spectrum of CQDs shows a very high carbon content relative to other elements. The presence of a substantial amount of oxygen in the CQDs EDS spectrum confirms the presence of oxygen-containing functionalities around the carbon dots. The other elements present in trace amounts are due to contamination during synthesis and handling prior to analysis. Noteworthy, the presence of Cu and O in the EDS spectrum of CuO confirmed the successful synthesis of copper oxide nanoparticles.

The electrochemical characterization of the modified electrodes was done in the presence of 10 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox probe in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7). The current response obtained from bare GCE, GCE/CuO, GCE/CQDs, and GCE/CQDs/CuO was 46.3, 18.6, 12.88 and 99.8 µA, respectively (Fig. 5 ). These results show that the CQDs and the CuO nanocomposite have succeeded in providing the best surface properties and electrical conductivity for the electron transfer between the redox probe and the modified electrode. Surprisingly, the bare electrode gave a better current response than the GCE/CQDs and GCE/CuO. This implies that the conductivity of these materials was not sufficient to individually foster better electron transfers kinetics than the bare electrode, probably due to the high film thickness of these materials, which adversely affected electron transfer in the redox probe. A similar occurrence was reported at a graphene oxide modified GCE in [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox probe, where the modified GCE gave a lower anodic current than the bare GCE 12. Using the Randle Sevcik equation (2) where ip, n, A, D, C and ν represent peak current (A), number of electrons transferred, electrode surface area (cm2), diffusion coefficient (cm2/s), concentration (mol cm−3) and scan rate (V/s), respectively,
(2)
i p
=
2.69
x
10
5
n
3 / 2
A D
1 / 2
C
ν
1 / 2

the electrode surface area recorded was 0.25, 0.10, 0.07, and 0.54 cm2 for bare GCE, GCE/CuO, GCE/CQDs, and GCE/CQDs/CuO, respectively. The larger effective surface area of the nanocomposite modified electrode relative to other electrodes, among other factors such as the synergistic effect of the CQDs and CuO in the composite, could be responsible for the significantly high oxidation current response at the nanocomposite modified electrode. In addition, the redox reaction of the redox probe at the nanocomposite-modified electrode occurred at a lower potential compared to every other electrode (Fig. 5). This implies that the redox reaction of the probe is much easier at this electrode and would be expected to equally catalyze biomolecules with redox properties to a much greater extent than other electrodes studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic response of CQDs/CuO to dopamine (DA) oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical response of the nanocomposite (CQDs/CuO) to DA was investigated in the presence of dopamine in PBS of pH 7.0 using cyclic voltammetry at 25 mV s−1 scan rate. As seen in Fig. 6 (A), the peak oxidation current response (potential in parenthesis) of DA at the bare GCE, CuO modified GCE (GCE/CuO), CQDs modified GCE (GCE/CQDs), and the CQDs/CuO nanocomposite modified GCE (GCE/CQDs/CuO) were 6.89 µA (0.21 V), 65.29 µA (0.26 V), 3.26 µA (0.24 V) and 345.5 µA (0.33 V), respectively. The oxidation peak current obtained at the GCE modified with the nanocomposite is about five and fifty times higher than that of bare GCE and GCE/CuO, respectively. This indicates that the combination of CuO and CQDs offered a synergy that culminated in a huge enhancement of the electrocatalytic activity of the composite towards the target molecule. Summarily, the current response of the electrodes to DA oxidation followed the order: GCE/CQDs/CuO > GCE/CuO > bare GCE > GCE/CQDs (Fig. 6B).

This result is a clear indication that the CQDs could not mediate fast electron transfer alone without the CuO nanoparticles’ support. Beyond this, it is also evident that GCE/CQDs had the poorest electrocatalytic activity towards DA, probably because of the low surface area of the as-synthesized CQDs, which culminated in poor DA oxidation at the electrode. The CQDs/CuO probably inherited its good electrocatalytic activity towards DA detection from the composite’s improved surface area and conductivity after introducing CuO nanoparticles into CQDs. Similar outstanding synergistic effects between carbon nanomaterials and metal oxide nanoparticles for DA detection have been reported in literature [11,12,25]. Aside from the synergy between the composite components, the introduction of CuO nanoparticles improved the surface area of the nanocomposite, coupled with the improved electrical conductivity contributed by the CuO nanoparticles. It could also be inferred from the SEM micrograph of the CQDs that there is a possibility that the CQDs have very little porosity, which was mitigated by introducing CuO nanoparticles. Therefore, it can be concluded that concerted conductivity and surface area of individual materials within the composite are responsible for the significant improvement in the electrocatalytic activity of the composite compared to its components. The superiority of GCE/CQDs/CuO towards DA oxidation made it the chosen candidate for further studies. Noteworthy, DA detection at this modified electrode is best achieved at a potential of about 0.3 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on DA oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of buffer pH on the electrocatalytic oxidation of DA was investigated by varying the concentration of the electrolyte from pH of 3–9. As seen in Fig. 8 , the highest anodic peak current (Iap) for DA was obtained at the pH of 7. This was closely followed by the high current response obtained at a pH of 3. The lowest current response was obtained at the pH of 9. Specifically, the current response at pH 7 is about 2.5 times what was obtained at pH 9. The significant difference between these two pH can be ascribed to the protonation of dopamine molecules at lower pH which consequently foster electrostatic interaction between the cationic DA and the oxygen containing functionalities on the CQDs. The cumulative effect of this electrostatic attraction and the π-π interaction between DA and the graphitic core within the CQDs could be responsible for the outstanding current response at pH of 7. In addition, adsorption of dopamine at pH 7 might be more favourable. Noteworthy, it has been reported that the adsorption of dopamine could be a limiting step in the electrochemical detection of DA [27]. On the contrary, no such protonation is feasible at very high pH and this encouraged possible repulsion between the DA molecule and the CQDs. Interestingly, the anodic peak potential (Eap) changes with pH followed the same trend as the peak current. The highest peak potential was equally obtained at a pH of 7. A pH of 7 was used for all electroanalytical studies because the highest current response was obtained at this pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of scan rate on DA oxidation at CQDs/CuO modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
The effect of scan rate on DA oxidation was investigated within the scan rate range of 25–400 mV s−1 in PBS at a pH of 7. Fig. 9 (A) shows that the oxidation peak current increased with an increase in the scan rate. The voltammograms also revealed a shift in the potential towards the right side with every increase in the scan rate. The plot of the oxidation peak current against the square root of the scan rate (v1/2) gave a linear plot with a correlation coefficient of 0.9865 (Fig. 9 B). The linear relationships between v1/2 and the peak currents (anodic (Iap) and cathodic (Icp)) were Iap (µA) = 164.799–21.489 v1/2 (mV s−1)1/2 (R2 = 0.9995) and Icp (µA) = -99.444 + 20.424 v1/2 (mV s−1)1/2 (R2 = 0.9989), respectively. A critical look at Fig. 9 B shows that the best linear fit between the peak currents and v1/2 occurred at higher scan rate (greater than200 mV). Linear relationships between the scan rate and the peak currents were also obtained (Fig. 9 C). This occurrence points to the fact that the oxidation of DA is both a diffusion-controlled and surface-confined process. A reaction mechanism of this nature has been reported in literature [24,28]. The electron transfer coefficient (
∝ )
was calculated from a cyclic voltammogram using the dependence of the anodic peak potential (Epa) on the natural logarithm of the scan rate (ln v) as described by Laviron. Noteworthy, Eqn (3) describes the linear relationship between Epa and ln v.
(3)
E
pa
m V
= 0.03873 + 0.03649 ln v
( m V
s
- 1
)

The number of electrons transferred coefficient (n) was obtained from Eqn (4), where R, T, and F represent the molar gas constant, absolute temperature, and Faraday’s constant, respectively. The slope of the plot of Epa against ln v (b) was used for obtaining the value of n. The value of n and ∝ obtained were 2.23 and 0.68, respectively. This value of n (∼2) means that two electrons were transferred during the redox process. The electron transfer rate constant (ks) was calculated from Eqn (5) as 0.27 cm s−1.
(4)
b = 2.3 R T / n ∝ F
(5)
log k s = ∝ log
1 - ∝
+
1 - ∝
l o g ∝ - log
RT
nFn
- ∝
1 - ∝
n F E
2.3 R T

In line with the electron transport process kinetics, a value of ks as high as 0.27 cm s−1 suggests that the reaction is reversible. This deduction is based on the premise that a ks value greater than 10-4 but less than 10-2 cm s−1 indicates a quasi-reversible reaction while a value beyond 10-2 suggests a reversible process [2]. This confirms the near-reversibility of the redox process earlier suspected from the Iap/Icp ratio (1.36) obtained from the CV of GCE/CQDs/CuO in dopamine (Fig. 6 A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dopamine redox reactions and scan rate changes: Experimental and theoretical studies
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of DA oxidation and the component of DA solution due to varying scan rates were investigated with UV–vis spectroscopy and DFT computational methods. The scan rate studies done with about 24 µM DA solution at a pH of 7 between a scan rate range of 25–300 mV s−1 showed that there is an increase in the current response with an increase in scan rate (graph not shown). This is in line with Ohms law because of the linear relationship between potential and current. The increase in current response with an increase in dopamine concentration has been documented [29–30]. Also, a low dopamine composition has shown strong compliance with Beer lambert’s law due to the linear relationship between DA absorbance and concentration recorded at 280 nm [31]. As confirmed by Fig. 10 A, the absorbance of dopamine taken at this wavelength (using PBS as solvent) increased with every increase in dopamine concentration. Surprisingly, the absorbance of the DA solution increased with an increase in scan rate (Fig. 10 B). This result was the product of the continuous measurement of the absorbance of the DA solution after every increase in scan rate from 25 to 300 mV s−1. Also, the absorbance of the DA solution taken before the first scan showed that the absorbance increased after the first scan at 25 mV s−1. Considering that the concentration of DA is expected to remain constant or reduce for every scan rate increase, it is expected that for every potential scan, there would have been a recovery of a part of the initial dopamine (if not all). Also, the high anodic peak current and the absorbance obtained for each scan rate imposed suggest that the amount of dopamine in solution relative to the quinone form increases with the scan rate increase. This result would have been conclusive if there was no increase in absorbance after the first scan rate (25 mV s−1) imposed. Therefore, it could be concluded that the absorbance recorded from each potential imposed (25–300 mV s−1) is a product of the interaction of a substance other than DA with UV light at 280 nm. This would be possible if DA and its oxidation product (dopamine-o-quinone) absorb at a similar wavelength.

Two substances with similar absorption wavelengths are expected to absorb the UV light at the same wavelength, albeit to a different extent because of the possible variation in molar extinction coefficient. The possible similarity in absorption wavelength of DA and its quinone form was investigated through a theoretical calculation of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) for both substances using the density functional theory (DFT) ran through the Gaussian 09 W software using the 6–31 + G basis set of the B3LYP hybrid functional. The quantum chemical parameters obtained were HOMO energy, LUMO energy, dipole moment (µ), and electronegativity (X). Fig. 11 shows the optimized structures for DA and its quinone form (Fig. 11 A and B) and the frontiers molecular orbital energy distribution (HOMO and LUMO) for DA (Fig. 11 C) and its quinone form (Fig. 11 D).

The theoretical calculations show that DA and the quinone form have different HOMO-LUMO gap (Table 2 ), which suggests they would be expected to have different absorption maxima under the UV light. This is in agreement with the absorption wavelength of 280 nm and 390 nm found in literature for DA and its quinone form, respectively [31,32]. This confirms that the absorbance measured at 280 nm is that of DA to a very large extent and possibly that of the quinone form to a much lower extent (due to weak absorption of UV by the quinone form at that wavelength). This also confirms that the variation in scan rate leads to the formation of DA and its quinone form in different proportions in solution after the redox process, especially at an electrode with adsorptive tendencies.

Also, the larger value of the HOMO energy (EHOMO) for dopamine suggests that it has a much higher electron-donating ability compared to the quinone form. On the other hand, the quinone form’s lower LUMO energy (ELUMO) shows its better electron acceptance attribute [33]. Consequently, DA releases electrons towards the electrode after imposing the potential while the quinone form accepts it during reduction. This agrees with the mechanism of dopamine oxidation described in literature (scheme 1 ) [28,30,34–37]. The electronegativity values (X) of both compounds, as seen in Table 2, also support this. The HOMO-LUMO energy gap (ΔE) for DA and the quinone form was calculated as 5.438 and 2.9892, respectively. The smaller the gap, the higher the possibility of the adsorption of the molecule to the CQDs/CuO modifier on the GCE [38]. This conclusion was drawn because a smaller ΔE value for the quinone form suggests that it is more stable than DA and, as such, might easily form an adduct with the CQDs/CuO modifier on the GCE surface. This explains why there was proof of adsorption on the modified electrode surface after each scan and, by extension, the lower value of the cathodic peak current relative to the anodic peak current (Fig. 6 A). Interestingly, the relationship between the scan rate changes and current response (Fig. 9) confirmed the possibility of this surface-confined adsorptive mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electroanalysis of DA at CQDs/CuO modified GCE
ce:section.ce:para:
The electroanalysis of dopamine at the modified electrode was done with square wave voltammetry (SWV) due to the better sensitivity of SWV than the cyclic voltammetry [2]. The effect of concentration on the electrode’s response to DA oxidation was investigated with SWV from a concentration range of 10 – 180 µM in PBS at a pH of 7.0. An increase in current response was observed with every increase in concentration (Fig. 12 ). The plot of the concentration against the current response gave a linear relationship;
I
μ A
= 3.27114 + 0.05611
D A
( μ M )
with a correlation coefficient of 0.99794. This relationship was linear over the concentration range of 10–180 µM. The limit of detection (LOD) calculated from the relationship between the relative standard deviation of intercept (δ) and the slope (m) of this linear relationship (LOD = 3.3 δ/m) was 25.4 µM. This detection limit favourably compares with the LOD obtained using some earlier fabricated sensors and other electroanalytical techniques (Table 3 ). This sensor has slightly higher LOD than some of those from literature, but the wide linear range surpasses that of some of these sensors. This suggests that the sensor is a potential instrument for accurate dopamine detection.

The interference study was done with the adoption of ascorbic acid (AA) as the interfering substance with DA. The poor AA-DA peak resolution offered by CV and the reputation of DPV for peak resolution, as confirmed by Fig. 13 (C) informed the decision to investigate AA interference with DA signal using DPV. It has been established that AA exists at a much higher concentration in biological systems than DA. Accordingly, 5 mM AA was used against 0.2 mM DA for the initial interference study (Fig. 13 B and C). Subsequently, AA concentration was kept constant (1 mM) while the DA concentration was increased from 100 µM to 125 µM (Fig. 13 D). The AA and DA peaks were found at 0.11 V and 0.25 V, respectively, on the differential pulse voltammogram (140 mV peak difference). It is evident from Fig. 13 (D) that the current response of DA increased with an increase in concentration. It was also observed that the AA peak weakened and outrightly disappeared with a higher concentration of DA. This phenomenon is an indication that the modified electrode might be highly selective for DA. The selectivity of the electrode might be due to the negative charge of the oxygen-containing functionalities on the CQDs, which attract the positively charged DA at physiological pH of 7. It could also be inferred that an increase in DA concentration reduced the chances of AA oxidation at the modified electrode since AA now has more favoured molecules to compete with.

Using the standard addition method, the real sample analysis was done by determining the DA content of diluted 200 mg/5 mL dopamine hydrochloride injection. The diluted DA injection ampoule was obtained by diluting 0.5 mL of the injection in 1000 folds. This diluted sample was spiked with known concentrations of DA. The amount of DA in the spiked samples was obtained from an initially prepared calibration curve, and then the percentage DA recovery by the electrode was calculated as reported by Adekunle et al [44]. The percentage recovery was 95.1 ± 6.2 % with RSD of 6.5 % (n = 3) (Table 4 ).

The long-term stability of the electrode was investigated through a cyclic voltammogram of 0.2 mM DA (Fig. 14 ). The oxidation peak current response obtained after 20 days of application of the modified electrode at a 5-days interval showed that the sensor was able to retain ca. 80 % of its initial current response. This shows that the electrode has relatively low stability compared to some sensors reported in literature where over 90 % of the current response was retained over a similar time-frame [1,3]. The reproducibility of the electrode was investigated by modifying three different electrodes and comparing the current response obtained from each electrode. The RSD of the current response obtained from these electrodes was 1.240 µA. This shows that the sensor is highly reproducible with minimal error margin.

In this study, we reported a sensor based on CQDs and CuO NPs nanocomposite for dopamine detection. The sensor shows a promising application for accurate dopamine detection considering its LOD and the wide linear range it possesses. In addition, the excellent recovery obtained from the application of this sensor for dopamine detection in dopamine hydrochloride injection suggests that this sensor is suitable for the detection of dopamine in pharmaceutical samples. Although the sensor suffered relatively low stability, other encouraging figures of merit are indications that the sensor can be commercialized for a point-of-care dopamine detection in pharmaceutical samples and other real samples containing dopamine down to the micromolar level.
All the authors contributed equally to the manuscript.

Saheed E. Elugoke: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Omolola E. Fayemi: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Abolanle S. Adekunle: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Bhekie B. Mamba: Writing – review & editing. Thabo T.I. Nkambule: Writing – review & editing. Eno E. Ebenso: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Software, Writing – review & editing, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Authors acknowledge the assistance of North-West University and the University of South Africa for providing facilities.
Data for the study is available upon reasonable request from the corresponding author.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical detection of dopamine based on functionalized electrodes
Coatings

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ouellette, M.; Mathault, J.; Niyonambaza, S. D.; Miled, A.; Boisselier, E., Electrochemical Detection of Dopamine Based on Functionalized Electrodes. Coatings 2019, 9 (8), 496.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive and selective dopamine biosensor fabricated with silanized graphene
J. Phys. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hou, S.; Kasner, M. L.; Su, S.; Patel, K.; Cuellari, R., Highly sensitive and selective dopamine biosensor fabricated with silanized graphene. The Journal of Physical Chemistry C 2010, 114 (35), 14915-14921.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conducting polyaniline-graphene oxide fibrous nanocomposites: preparation, characterization and simultaneous electrochemical detection of ascorbic acid, dopamine and uric acid
RSC Adv.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Manivel, P.; Dhakshnamoorthy, M.; Balamurugan, A.; Ponpandian, N.; Mangalaraj, D.; Viswanathan, C., Conducting polyaniline-graphene oxide fibrous nanocomposites: preparation, characterization and simultaneous electrochemical detection of ascorbic acid, dopamine and uric acid. Rsc Advances 2013, 3 (34), 14428-14437.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous voltammetric determination of ascorbic acid and dopamine at the surface of electrodes modified with self-assembled gold nanoparticle films
J. Solid State Electrochem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Raoof, J. B.; Kiani, A.; Ojani, R.; Valiollahi, R.; Rashid-Nadimi, S., Simultaneous voltammetric determination of ascorbic acid and dopamine at the surface of electrodes modified with self-assembled gold nanoparticle films. Journal of Solid State Electrochemistry 2010, 14 (7), 1171-1176.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: L.V. da Silva, A.K. de Almeida, J.A. Xavier, C.B. Lopes, F.d.A. dos Santos Silva, P.R. Lima, N.D. dos Santos, L.T. Kubota, M.O. Goulart, Phenol based redox mediators in electroanalysis. J. Electroanal. Chem. 2018, 827, 230-252.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrocatalytic detection of dopamine at single-walled carbon nanotubes–iron (III) oxide nanoparticles platform
Sens. Actuators, B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Adekunle, A. S.; Agboola, B. O.; Pillay, J.; Ozoemena, K. I., Electrocatalytic detection of dopamine at single-walled carbon nanotubes–iron (III) oxide nanoparticles platform. Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical 2010, 148 (1), 93-102.
